findings.title: No code produces the explainability result (Figure 4, MNIST digit, AUROC 0.76)
findings.quote:
(3) Explainability: TCCM enables feature-level
attribution for anomaly scores, supporting interpretable diagnosis—an aspect largely absent in
existing deep anomaly detection models (see Figure 4).
findings.claim: Explainability is one of the paper's four headline contributions (RQ3); Figure 4 shows feature-level anomaly explanations on MNIST with a reported AUROC of 0.76, but a repo-wide grep for any explanation/visualization/MNIST-image script returns nothing (see _audit_code/out/missing_artifacts.txt).
findings.concern: A headline contribution (feature-level explainability) and its figure/number cannot be reproduced because the code that generates them is absent from the repository.
findings.resolution: Authors: please add the script that produces Figure 4 (the MNIST digit-1 explanation and the 0.76 AUROC) and the feature-attribution visualization.
findings.reason: Paper quote confirmed at paper_text.txt lines 134-136; repo-wide grep over all .py files for 'explain', 'mnist', 'MNIST', 'attribution', 'visualiz', 'Figure4' finds no script that produces Figure 4 or the 0.76 AUROC, confirming the code is absent.

findings.title: Unsupervised epoch-selection method not in repo; only hardcoded epoch results
findings.claim: The paper states the number of training epochs is chosen per-dataset via 'the unsupervised hyperparameter selection method proposed by Li et al. (2025b), which requires no access to anomaly labels'; the repo contains only the resulting hardcoded epoch values (e.g. comments such as 'Chosen first from "100 or 1"' at lines 128/132), not the selection procedure itself.
findings.concern: The per-dataset epoch values—the only data-dependent TCCM hyperparameter—cannot be reproduced and the label-free claim cannot be verified, because the selection code that would produce these numbers without anomaly labels is absent.
findings.resolution: Authors: please include the implementation (or a runnable wrapper) of the unsupervised epoch-selection method so the Table 5 epoch values are reproducible and the no-label criterion is checkable.
findings.paper_ref: Appendix B.3, 'The number of training epochs is determined ... unsupervised hyperparameter selection method'
findings.reason: Code quote confirmed at FMAD/functions.py lines 73-80 (exact match including 'epoch_size = 200' for backdoor); paper_text.txt line 2662-2663 confirms the Li et al. 2025b CSM criterion claim; grep for 'CSM', 'Contrast.*Score', 'Margin', 'unsupervised.*select' across all .py files returns nothing.

findings.title: Figures 16, 17 and Autoencoder+TimeEmbedding ablations claimed but absent from code
findings.quote:
(4) Feature normalization (Figure 16): z-score normalization is generally beneficial and improves robustness; (5) Time-interpolated
inputs (Figure 17): interpolation offers no gain and may add noise; (6) Comparison with Autoencoder
+Time Embedding: confirms that TCCM learns a time-conditioned velocity field rather than a
reconstruction mapping.
findings.claim: The paper lists six ablation/sensitivity studies; AblationStudies.py implements Figures 12-15 only. A repo-wide grep finds no code for Figure 16 (feature normalization), Figure 17 (time-interpolated inputs), or the Autoencoder+Time-Embedding comparison (see _audit_code/out/missing_artifacts.txt). Note the 'interpolated_x' at AblationStudies.py:46-47 belongs to the noise-injection study (Fig. 14), not a Fig. 17 study.
findings.concern: Three of the paper's claimed ablation analyses have no reproducing code, so those conclusions (e.g. 'z-score normalization improves robustness', 'interpolation offers no gain') cannot be verified from the repo.
findings.resolution: Authors: please add the scripts that produce the Figure 16, Figure 17, and Autoencoder+Time-Embedding ablation results.
findings.reason: Paper quote confirmed at paper_text.txt lines 1164-1166; AblationStudies.py save paths show only Figures 12-14 (Time_Embedding_Figure_12, Sensitivity_t_Figure_13, Noise_Injection_Figure_14); no grep match for 'Figure_16', 'Figure_17', 'normalization ablation', 'time.interp', or 'Autoencoder' producing a study in any .py file.

findings.title: run_knn.sh invokes nonexistent Full_experiments.py (actual file is FullExperiments.py)
findings.claim: The helper script run_knn.sh calls `python Full_experiments.py`, but the repository file is `FullExperiments.py` (camel-case); `Full_experiments.py` does not exist (verified by ls), so every invocation in this script fails with a file-not-found error.
findings.concern: The standalone KNN reproduction script cannot run as written; it errors immediately on the wrong filename (low impact since KNN is also runnable through the main FullExperiments.py model index).
findings.resolution: Rename the invoked file to `FullExperiments.py` in run_knn.sh, or remove the unused helper script.
findings.reason: Quote confirmed at run_knn.sh line 28; `ls` of repo root confirms only `FullExperiments.py` exists, not `Full_experiments.py`, so the invocation fails as claimed.

findings.title: requirements.txt pins torch==1.13.1 but paper and README state PyTorch 2.0
findings.claim: requirements.txt pins torch==1.13.1, whereas Appendix B.3 of the paper states 'Our implementation is based on Python 3.9.21 with PyTorch 2.0'. Both are internally valid PyTorch versions for this MLP; only the recorded environment differs.
findings.concern: The reported software environment (PyTorch 2.0) does not match the pinned dependency (1.13.1), a minor reproducibility inconsistency that could change low-order numerical results.
findings.resolution: Authors: confirm which PyTorch version produced the reported numbers and align requirements.txt with the paper's stated environment.
findings.reason: Quote confirmed at requirements.txt line 7 (`torch==1.13.1`); paper_text.txt line 2686 confirms 'PyTorch 2.0' statement; discrepancy is factual.

findings.title: TCCM gets per-dataset epoch tuning while all baselines use default hyperparameters
findings.claim: TCCM is configured with per-dataset tuned epochs (and lr/batch) via determine_FMAD_hyperparameters, while all baselines run with default configurations (the paper confirms 'For all baseline detectors, we use their default configurations and hyperparameters'); only MCM/GOAD get a single fixed param, none get per-dataset tuning.
findings.concern: Tuning a key training-budget hyperparameter per dataset for the proposed method only—while deep baselines keep defaults—can bias the head-to-head ranking comparison (Figure 2) in TCCM's favour; the asymmetry's fairness hinges on the epoch-selection truly using no anomaly labels, which is unverifiable because the selection code is absent (see unsupervised-epoch-selection-not-implemented).
findings.resolution: Authors: either apply an equivalent label-free per-dataset selection budget to the deep baselines, or report TCCM with a single fixed epoch budget to show the ranking is robust to this asymmetry; and release the selection code so the label-free claim is checkable.
findings.paper_ref: Appendix B.3 (epochs via unsupervised selection; baselines use defaults)
findings.reason: Code quote confirmed at FullExperiments.py lines 254-266 (exact match); TCCM uniquely calls determine_FMAD_hyperparameters while baselines receive fixed or empty params, confirming the asymmetry claim.
